lists.label: Accessibility Links and Resources

pedagogy.philosophy: A senior UX professional with Google and Microsoft pedigree learns by doing, not by reading. This approach frames every resource and concept as ammunition for a specific consulting scenario — client objections, audit deliverables, pitch decks, vendor disputes. The app is structured as a series of realistic consulting situations the learner must navigate, with the curriculum surfacing in context as the tools, references, and arguments needed to handle each situation credibly. Retention is driven not by spaced repetition flashcards but by the emotional salience of professional stakes: the learner always knows exactly why they are learning this right now, because the scenario makes the cost of not knowing viscerally clear. This respects the learner's existing mastery by treating them as a consultant-in-training rather than a student, and it builds the social currency and rhetorical fluency they explicitly want alongside the technical depth.

appStructure.primaryOrganization: Eight consulting scenarios mapped one-to-one with curriculum modules, each scenario presenting a realistic client situation that can only be resolved by engaging with that module's resources. Scenarios are unlocked sequentially but each contains branching decision points that allow non-linear exploration within the scenario. A persistent 'Practice Toolkit' accumulates every resource, argument, and checklist the learner has successfully deployed, becoming a personal reference artifact they can use in real engagements. A ninth meta-scenario — 'Your First RFP' — is unlocked only after all eight are completed and synthesizes the full curriculum into a proposal writing challenge.
appStructure.sessionUnit: One consulting scenario act, defined as a single decision point and its immediate consequences, estimated at 15-25 minutes. Each full scenario contains three to five acts. A session always ends at an act boundary, never mid-act, so the learner always leaves with a resolved micro-narrative and a clear sense of what they handled.
retentionMechanisms: Scenario replay with variant conditions: after completing a scenario, the learner can replay it with a changed client context (e.g., the nonprofit now has a federal grant requirement instead of donor pressure) requiring different resource selection and argument framing — surface-level content is the same but application logic must shift

The 'Objection Dojo': at the end of each module scenario, a rapid-fire client objection round presents five common pushbacks (e.g., 'we already use an overlay, why do we need more?') and the learner must select the most persuasive and accurate response from three options, with immediate feedback citing the specific resource that grounds the correct answer

Toolkit annotation: every resource added to the Practice Toolkit prompts the learner to write a one-sentence 'when I would use this' note in their own words — this generative retrieval practice creates a personalized reference that is more memorable than the original resource description

Cross-module connection surfacing: when a resource appears in multiple scenarios, the app highlights the connection explicitly ('You used WebAIM Million data in Module 2 to justify investment — here in Module 5 it reappears as audit credibility evidence'), building a semantic web rather than isolated module silos

Peer pressure simulation: a 'What would a credible consultant say?' moment appears once per scenario where the learner must draft a two-to-three sentence client response in their own words before seeing a model answer — the comparison drives self-assessment without external grading

Freshness flags surfaced in context: resources marked with freshnessFlag:true are presented with an explicit 'verify before using in a client deliverable' prompt at the moment they are deployed in a scenario, training the habit of currency-checking rather than teaching it abstractly

The 'Gaps Radar': a persistent sidebar visualization showing the eight notable curriculum gaps as named blind spots that have not yet been addressed in any scenario — as the learner progresses, this remains partially unfilled, modeling intellectual honesty and creating motivation to seek supplemental resources
appStructure.navigationModel: Linear scenario progression at the macro level, enforcing the dependency logic of the curriculum (you cannot advise on WCAG before understanding why it exists). Within each scenario, branching is free: the learner chooses which client question to address first, which resource to consult, which argument to lead with. Branches converge at act resolution so no branch is a dead end — wrong choices produce realistic negative consequences (the client is skeptical, the proposal is rejected, the overlay vendor gets the contract) followed by a debrief explaining what a stronger choice would have been. A global map view shows all eight scenarios as a project timeline, visually reinforcing the consulting workflow metaphor.
progressIndicators: Scenario completion arcs: each scenario shows a five-segment progress bar labeled with the acts (Discovery, Diagnosis, Recommendation, Objection Handling, Deliverable), filling as acts are completed
Practice Toolkit resource count with annotation completeness percentage — you have added 14 of 33 resources, 9 annotations written
Objection Dojo accuracy score per module, displayed as a percentage and color-coded by confidence tier (below 60% red, 60-80% amber, above 80% green)
Replay badge: scenarios that have been replayed with variant conditions show a distinguishing visual marker signaling depth of engagement beyond first pass

The 'Gaps Radar' fill state: as the learner voluntarily logs supplemental resources addressing named gaps, the radar partially fills — a qualitative signal of intellectual curiosity beyond the core curriculum

Cumulative consulting credibility score: a lightly gamified aggregate of objection accuracy, toolkit annotation quality (measured by word count as a proxy for engagement), and replay completeness — framed not as a grade but as a client-readiness signal, displayed on the global map as a readiness percentage

designDirectives: Visual language must read as a professional work tool, not an e-learning platform — use the aesthetic vocabulary of a well-designed internal consulting toolkit or strategy dashboard (muted palette, dense typographic hierarchy, data-table patterns) rather than the bright colors and rounded cards of consumer education apps; the learner should feel they are using a work instrument

Each scenario must open with a concrete client artifact — an email thread, a Slack message, a grant requirement clause, a vendor sales deck excerpt — that establishes the situation before any instructional content appears; the artifact should be designed with enough realistic detail to be uncomfortable in its specificity

Resource cards within scenarios must never be presented as a reading list; they must appear only at the moment the scenario logic creates a need for them, framed as 'you need to answer this question — here is the authoritative source', and the card must show the whyThisWhyNow text as the primary frame, with the resource description as secondary detail

The Practice Toolkit must be designed as a genuinely usable reference artifact that the learner would want to export and bring to a real client engagement — consider a clean print or PDF export mode with the learner's own annotations as the primary content and the resource metadata as supporting structure

Branching decision points must present options as realistic professional choices with plausible-sounding wrong answers, never as obvious trick questions — a learner with senior UX experience will disengage immediately if the choices feel like they are being talked down to; wrong options should represent genuinely defensible but suboptimal professional instincts

The Objection Dojo interaction must be time-pressured with a visible but non-punishing countdown (30 seconds) to simulate the social reality of being challenged in a client meeting — the time pressure is the mechanism that makes the retention salient, not the score

Freshness flags must be rendered as a distinct visual treatment — a warning amber timestamp badge on the resource card — that is visually consistent across every place the resource appears, so the learner develops an automatic visual habit of noticing currency signals in the wild

The ninth meta-scenario (Your First RFP) must present a blank proposal document template that the learner fills in using only resources and arguments from their completed Practice Toolkit — the interaction is open-ended and the feedback is a structured self-assessment rubric, not an automated score, respecting the learner's professional judgment

The Gaps Radar must be designed with enough visual prominence and honest labeling — using the exact gap names from the curriculum JSON notableGaps array — that the learner never finishes the curriculum believing they have achieved comprehensive competence; intellectual humility is a feature, not a bug

Mobile layout must be fully functional but the primary design target is a 1280px desktop viewport, reflecting the reality that consulting prep work happens at a desk; do not sacrifice information density in the desktop experience in service of a mobile-first constraint that does not match the learner's actual context

All scenario debrief screens must include a single 'What an expert would say' pull quote attributed to a named practitioner from the curriculum (Lainey Feingold, Adrian Roselli, Meryl Evans, etc.) — this builds parasocial familiarity with the community voices the learner will eventually need to cite by name in client contexts

The global map view must use a project timeline metaphor explicitly — numbered phases with connecting lines, milestone markers, an estimated time-to-completion counter — so the learner internalizes the curriculum as a professional development project they are managing, not a course they are taking

modules.moduleTitle: Foundations: why accessibility matters and who it serves
modules.moduleDescription: Establishes the moral, legal, business, and user-centered case for accessibility before touching any implementation detail. For a consultant building a nonprofit practice, this module provides the rhetorical and conceptual grounding needed to make the case to low-budget clients who need persuasion before they need a checklist.

resources.label: Web Accessibility Initiative – Fundamentals Overview
resources.description: A comprehensive overview page from W3C WAI providing starting resources for learning web accessibility, curating foundational materials including introduction to accessibility, real-world user perspectives, essential components, accessibility principles, and free online courses.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The canonical entry point to accessibility rationale from the standards body itself — gives your client decks an authoritative, citable foundation that commands instant credibility in any boardroom or grant proposal.
keyTopics: How people with disabilities use the web

resources.label: A11y 101
resources.description: An introductory web accessibility guide designed for both designers and developers, providing foundational knowledge on making websites accessible with practical guidance on design principles, development practices, and the legal and business case for accessibility.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Bridges the gap between abstract principle and concrete practice in a single readable sweep — ideal background reading before your first nonprofit discovery call when the client conflates accessibility with aesthetics.
keyTopics: Legal and business case for accessibility

resources.label: W3C – World Wide Web Consortium
resources.description: The international standards organization that develops specifications and guidelines for an accessible, internationalized, private, and secure web, serving businesses, researchers, and developers across industries.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Understanding the standards body behind WCAG gives you organizational fluency — essential when advising nonprofits on procurement language, grant compliance requirements, or international digital equity programs.

resources.label: Law Office of Lainey Feingold – Digital Accessibility Ethics & Structured Negotiation
resources.description: Lainey Feingold's professional site showcasing her work as a disability rights lawyer specializing in digital accessibility and Structured Negotiation — a collaborative dispute resolution approach that avoids litigation.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Structured Negotiation is precisely the low-conflict, relationship-preserving model that nonprofits and NGOs need — learning this framework lets you position accessibility remediation as partnership rather than legal threat.
resources.freshnessNote: Most recent articles dated March 2026 — actively maintained. Legal landscape around ADA Title II is shifting rapidly in 2025–2026; treat any pre-2024 case citations with caution and verify currency.

resources.label: Overlay Fact Sheet
resources.description: A detailed critical analysis of web accessibility overlay technologies — their strengths, weaknesses, privacy implications, inability to achieve true WCAG compliance, and documented harm to users with disabilities, with testimonials from disabled users and a statement from industry signatories.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Nonprofit clients with tight budgets are prime overlay sales targets — this fact sheet arms you with the community consensus and user evidence needed to steer clients away from snake-oil solutions before they waste money and create legal exposure.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit executive director pushes back on your accessibility proposal saying, 'We're a small org — we've never been sued and our users haven't complained.' Which argument is most strategically effective for this specific client context?
options: Cite ADA Title II regulations and explain that the DOJ's 2024 rule updates now explicitly cover web content, creating real litigation exposure even for small nonprofits receiving federal funding.
Explain that 26% of U.S. adults have a disability and inaccessible sites exclude potential donors and beneficiaries, directly undermining the org's stated mission.
Describe how structured negotiation — a pre-litigation resolution process favored by disability rights advocates — can result in costly agreements that bypass formal lawsuits entirely.
Show the WebAIM Million survey data proving that 96% of home pages have detectable WCAG failures, meaning their site almost certainly has issues whether or not complaints have surfaced.
quizQuestions.explanation: Mission alignment is the highest-leverage argument for a nonprofit audience because it reframes accessibility as integral to organizational purpose rather than a compliance burden — making it harder to deprioritize on budget grounds. Legal and data arguments are secondary ammunition once mission resonance is established.

quizQuestions.question: Your nonprofit client has just deployed an accessibility overlay, citing the vendor's claim that it achieves 'instant WCAG compliance.' How do you advise them?
options: Accept the overlay as a reasonable interim measure but document that manual testing is still required to confirm conformance claims before making any public compliance statement.
Recommend they keep the overlay for automated repair of low-stakes issues but commission a full manual audit for their donation and service intake flows.

Advise them to remove the overlay immediately because overlays introduce privacy risks by injecting third-party scripts, do not remediate underlying code, and have been publicly repudiated by disabled users and legal experts.
Suggest they request the vendor's VPAT documentation and evaluate overlay performance against screen reader compatibility before making a decision.
quizQuestions.explanation: Overlays do not fix source-code barriers, create privacy vulnerabilities through third-party JavaScript, and have been widely criticized by the disability community — meaning they can increase legal and reputational risk rather than reduce it. A consultant with this knowledge protects clients from a predatory vendor category, which is itself a differentiator.

quizQuestions.question: A foundation program officer asks whether your accessibility audit scope should follow WCAG compliance requirements or inclusive design principles. How do you frame the relationship between the two for a funder audience?
options: Explain that WCAG compliance and inclusive design are effectively synonymous — achieving AA conformance on all pages constitutes an inclusive design practice.
Position WCAG compliance as the legal floor that prevents liability, while inclusive design is the ceiling that centers disabled users' actual experience — a credible practice addresses both layers.
Advise the funder that inclusive design principles are aspirational and not measurable, so the audit scope should focus exclusively on WCAG success criteria to produce defensible deliverables.
Recommend scoping the audit to WCAG AA only, since inclusive design principles are not codified in U.S. law and therefore outside a compliance-oriented engagement.
quizQuestions.explanation: Framing WCAG as floor and inclusive design as ceiling accurately represents the relationship and signals consulting sophistication — it also expands engagement scope, since a compliance-only audit is a commodity service whereas process and design integration is not.

modules.moduleTitle: The WCAG standard: reading it, using it, citing it
modules.moduleDescription: Deep-dives the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines as a living technical standard — not just as a compliance checklist but as a design framework you can teach and apply. For a senior UX professional pivoting to consulting, fluency with WCAG levels, success criteria, and supporting techniques is the single most leverage-generating investment you can make.

resources.label: WCAG 2 at a Glance
resources.description: A paraphrased plain-language summary of WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 organized around the four foundational POUR principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The POUR framework is the conceptual scaffold your clients and their teams will actually remember — start here before diving into success criteria so the detailed spec lands in an existing mental model.

resources.label: WCAG 2 Overview – Web Accessibility Initiative
resources.description: An authoritative overview of WCAG 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2, explaining how to make web content accessible, the three conformance levels, 13 guidelines, and supporting materials including Quick Reference and Techniques.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: This is the primary citation you'll use in every audit report, proposal, and client contract — understanding the versioning history and backwards compatibility is non-negotiable when advising on procurement or legal compliance.
resources.freshnessNote: Updated October 2025 — critically important to stay current as WCAG 2.2 is now the live normative standard and WCAG 3.0 is in active development; any guidance referencing only WCAG 2.0 may be outdated for current compliance conversations.

resources.label: A11y Project Checklist
resources.description: A comprehensive task-based accessibility checklist grounded in WCAG 2.2 standards, covering 18 categories of web accessibility concerns with specific, testable success criteria for each item.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The most field-trusted practitioner-facing distillation of WCAG into actionable audit items — use it as the operational backbone of your consulting deliverables without reinventing the wheel.

resources.label: WebAIM – Web Accessibility In Mind
resources.description: A university-based nonprofit providing comprehensive web accessibility expertise through training, consulting, evaluation services, and free tools, with the annual WebAIM Million survey tracking the state of accessibility across the top 1 million websites.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: WebAIM's annual Million report is the most-cited data source in accessibility industry conversations — knowing these numbers cold makes you a more credible voice when justifying accessibility investments to nonprofit leadership.

quizQuestions.question: During an audit debrief, a nonprofit's developer argues that a failing form element is 'close enough' because it passes WCAG 2.0 AA even though it fails a 2.1 AA success criterion. The org's legal counsel cites only WCAG 2.0 in their procurement contracts. How do you advise the client?
options: Agree with the developer — if the procurement contract specifies WCAG 2.0, the 2.1 failure is out of scope and should be logged as a recommendation, not a finding.

Recommend updating the procurement contract language to WCAG 2.1 AA, because 2.1 is backwards-compatible with 2.0 and closing the gap eliminates both current user-experience failures and forward legal exposure.
Escalate to WCAG 2.2 as the current published standard and advise the client that any version below 2.2 is technically outdated and inadvisable to cite in contracts.
Flag the 2.1 failure as a priority finding regardless of contract language, because WCAG 2.1 is an ISO/IEC 40500 standard and supersedes contractual references to 2.0 under international law.
quizQuestions.explanation: WCAG 2.1 is a strict superset of 2.0 (backwards-compatible), so upgrading the procurement reference costs nothing technically while closing real user-experience gaps and aligning with current regulatory expectations. This is a concrete, low-friction recommendation that demonstrates both technical and contractual fluency.

quizQuestions.question: A client asks why their automated scanner shows 0 WCAG AA errors but disabled users are still reporting difficulty using the site. Which explanation most accurately reflects what WCAG success criteria can and cannot measure?
options: The scanner is likely misconfigured — a properly tuned automated tool should detect any failure mode that WCAG AA success criteria cover.

WCAG AA success criteria are binary pass/fail rules that can be fully evaluated by automated tools; if the scanner passes the site, the user reports likely reflect preference rather than access barriers.

Automated tools reliably catch only 20–30% of WCAG issues; many AA success criteria — such as 2.4.6 Headings and Labels or 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions — require human judgment to evaluate meaningfully.
The WebAIM Million methodology is the most comprehensive automated evaluation available, and a clean result from that benchmark is a reasonable proxy for actual user accessibility.
quizQuestions.explanation: Automated tools catch a well-documented minority of WCAG failures; criteria under Understandable and Operable principles especially require contextual human evaluation. Understanding this gap is what positions a consultant above automated-report resellers.

quizQuestions.question: A client's design team wants to know whether they need to meet WCAG AAA criteria to avoid legal risk. How do you accurately characterize AAA in your response?
options: AAA conformance is legally required for healthcare and government-funded nonprofits under Section 508 and ADA Title II, so the team should treat it as a compliance floor for those verticals.

AAA criteria are aspirational enhancements not required for any recognized legal standard, but selectively pursuing high-impact AAA criteria — such as 1.4.6 Enhanced Contrast or 2.2.3 No Timing — can significantly improve experience for users with low vision or cognitive disabilities.
AAA conformance is identical to AA but adds a layer of certification; organizations can claim it after a third-party audit confirms zero failures across all 78 success criteria.
AAA criteria are deprecated in WCAG 2.2 and have been replaced by new AA criteria, so advising clients to pursue them is no longer considered best practice.
quizQuestions.explanation: No legal standard currently mandates AAA conformance, but a consultant who can identify which AAA criteria deliver outsized accessibility benefit for a client's specific audience adds strategic value beyond checkbox compliance. This nuance separates a design-informed consultant from a compliance auditor.

modules.moduleTitle: Semantic HTML, ARIA, and the accessibility tree
modules.moduleDescription: Covers the technical underpinnings of accessible web interfaces — from semantic HTML as the first line of defence to ARIA as a precision tool for complex components. A senior UX professional advising development teams needs enough technical fluency to review code, brief engineers credibly, and spot misuse of ARIA that creates more problems than it solves.

resources.label: HTML: HyperText Markup Language – MDN
resources.description: MDN's comprehensive HTML reference and tutorial hub covering elements, attributes, semantic markup, forms, responsive images, and content structure with accessibility implications throughout.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: When a client's dev team says 'we used a div for everything,' you need enough HTML semantics fluency to articulate why that matters — MDN is the reference you'll return to constantly to ground those conversations.

resources.label: HTML5 Accessibility – Browser Support State
resources.description: A reference tool that tests and reports on HTML5 feature accessibility support across major browsers, evaluating keyboard accessibility, platform accessibility API mapping, and whether ARIA workarounds are needed.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Before recommending a specific HTML element to a client's dev team, you need to know if it actually works across the AT stack their users rely on — this is the ground-truth cross-browser reality check.
resources.freshnessNote: Editor's draft dated August 2020 — browser support landscape has shifted significantly since then; cross-check critical elements against a11ysupport.io for current data.

resources.label: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG)
resources.description: The W3C's comprehensive guide teaching developers how to use ARIA roles, states, and properties to create accessible web components and widgets, with design patterns, functional examples, keyboard support guidance, and accessible naming conventions.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The APG is the authoritative reference when reviewing or writing accessibility requirements for complex UI components like modals, carousels, and data grids — fluency here elevates you from checklist auditor to genuine technical collaborator.

resources.label: Accessibility Support – Will your code work with assistive technologies?
resources.description: A community-driven database testing how well ARIA roles, attributes, and HTML elements work across multiple assistive technologies including screen readers, voice control, and speech recognition, providing real-world compatibility data.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The gap between 'ARIA says this should work' and 'this actually works for JAWS users on Windows' is where many accessibility implementations fail — this database lets you make evidence-based recommendations rather than theoretical ones.

resources.label: WAI-ARIA Screen Reader Compatibility – PowerMapper
resources.description: Comprehensive compatibility testing data for WAI-ARIA attributes and roles across major screen readers (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) and browsers, showing reliability percentages with historical trends since 2014.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: When a client asks 'does this work for blind users?', historical reliability trends help you set realistic expectations and prioritize remediation effort where AT failure rates are highest.
resources.freshnessNote: Updated December 2025 — relatively current, but always cross-reference with a11ysupport.io as the ARIA spec and AT implementations update frequently.

resources.label: Adrian Roselli – Blog
resources.description: Adrian Roselli's in-depth blog covering ARIA implementation, WCAG compliance, screen reader support, accessibility overlays, and critical analysis of vendor accessibility claims, aimed at accessibility professionals and web developers.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Roselli is one of the most technically rigorous voices in the community and regularly exposes gaps between vendor claims and real-world AT behavior — following his work keeps your recommendations peer-reviewed by the field's most demanding critic.

quizQuestions.question: You're reviewing a React component library built by a nonprofit's agency vendor. The agency used div and span elements throughout, adding ARIA roles to approximate semantic behavior. How do you brief the client on the risk?
options: Advise that ARIA roles on div elements are fully equivalent to native semantic HTML and the implementation is sound, provided the roles are correctly named and mapped.

Explain that while ARIA can approximate semantics, native HTML elements carry built-in keyboard support, browser accessibility API mappings, and cross-AT compatibility that ARIA-on-div implementations must manually recreate — creating ongoing maintenance debt and reliability risk.

Recommend the client commission a full JAWS and NVDA compatibility audit before making any architectural recommendations, since ARIA support varies enough that you cannot advise without empirical AT testing data.
Flag the pattern as a WCAG 4.1.2 failure and advise immediate remediation of all interactive components before any user testing is conducted.
quizQuestions.explanation: The first rule of ARIA is not to use ARIA when a native HTML element exists — native elements carry implicit roles, states, and keyboard behaviors that ARIA-on-div patterns must manually implement and maintain. This argument gives the client a principled architectural recommendation rather than a compliance citation.

quizQuestions.question: A developer on your client's team argues that adding aria-label to every icon button is sufficient to make the interface screen-reader accessible. What is the most important gap in this approach you need to surface?
options: aria-label alone does not establish an accessible name on icon buttons — the button element must also include role='button' explicitly for screen readers to announce it correctly.

aria-label provides the accessible name but does not communicate state — icon buttons that toggle (mute/unmute, expand/collapse) also require aria-pressed or aria-expanded so screen reader users understand current context without sighted reference.
The approach is correct for JAWS and NVDA but fails on VoiceOver because iOS VoiceOver ignores aria-label on button elements in favor of the button's text content.
aria-label is deprecated in WCAG 2.2 and should be replaced with aria-labelledby pointing to a visually hidden span element for forwards compatibility.
quizQuestions.explanation: Accessible names without accessible states leave screen reader users knowing what a control is called but not what it is currently doing — a critical gap for toggle controls. Surfacing this distinction demonstrates that you understand ARIA's name-role-value model, not just its labeling mechanics.

quizQuestions.question: During a code review you spot a modal dialog that uses aria-hidden='true' on the background content but does not trap keyboard focus inside the modal. A junior developer says the aria-hidden is enough because screen readers won't read the background. How do you respond?
options: Agree — aria-hidden on the background content is the WCAG-specified technique for modal dialogs and keyboard focus management is a nice-to-have rather than a conformance requirement.

Explain that aria-hidden removes background content from the accessibility tree for screen readers but does nothing to prevent keyboard users — including screen reader users navigating by Tab — from moving focus outside the modal, which breaks the ARIA dialog design pattern and creates a WCAG 2.1.2 failure.
Recommend they also add role='dialog' to the modal container, which automatically enables browser-native focus trapping and resolves the keyboard navigation issue without additional JavaScript.
Flag this as a WCAG 1.3.1 failure because the relationship between the modal and its trigger button is not programmatically determinable without focus management in place.
quizQuestions.explanation: aria-hidden handles the perception layer for AT users but focus management is a separate, required behavior — the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide dialog pattern explicitly requires focus to be trapped within the modal. Knowing that these are independent problems prevents a false sense of completeness from a partial fix.

modules.moduleTitle: Rich media accessibility: images, captions, and audio description
modules.moduleDescription: Addresses the accessibility of non-text content — images, video, and audio — which represents a majority of WCAG failures on real-world sites. For a consultant whose nonprofit clients produce mission-critical visual storytelling and video content, this module is immediately billable: every org with a YouTube channel or photo-heavy impact report has work to do here.

resources.label: Alt Text as Poetry
resources.description: A collaborative project by disabled artists that reframes alt text as a form of creative practice, providing a free workbook with writing exercises and exploring how thoughtful image description benefits blind and low-vision users while enriching digital experiences for all.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Nonprofits produce emotionally resonant visual content that deserves equally resonant description — this resource reframes alt text from a compliance checkbox into an act of storytelling that aligns with mission-driven organizations' values.
keyTopics: Alt text as accessibility and poetry

resources.label: The Complete Guide to Captioning Videos – Meryl Evans
resources.description: A comprehensive guide by deaf accessibility expert Meryl Evans covering why captions matter, 10 best practices for high-quality captions, usage statistics, and an extensive directory of captioning tools and services for content creators.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: 86% of caption users aren't deaf — that statistic alone reframes captioning as audience expansion for nonprofits whose video content is central to fundraising and advocacy, making this one of your highest-ROI talking points with clients.

resources.label: Live WebVTT Validator
resources.description: An interactive browser-based validator for WebVTT files used in video captions, descriptions, and metadata, providing real-time validation feedback with specific error locations.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: When auditing a nonprofit's video library, malformed caption files are a common and easily fixable finding — having this validator in your toolkit lets you demonstrate and fix problems on the spot during client walkthroughs.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit's communications team wrote the following alt text for a photo in their annual impact report: 'Image of people at an event.' As their accessibility consultant, what is the most useful critique you can offer?
options: The alt text is acceptable for decorative images but should be replaced with an empty alt attribute (alt='') since impact report photos are primarily aesthetic.

The alt text fails because it begins with 'Image of' — screen readers already announce the element as an image, making that prefix redundant — and it conveys no information about who the people are, what is happening, or why this image was selected to illustrate impact.
The alt text is technically WCAG 1.1.1 compliant because it provides a non-empty text alternative; the team should focus remediation effort on images that have no alt text at all.
Recommend replacing the alt text with the photo caption verbatim, since captions are reviewed by the communications team for accuracy and serve the same informational purpose.
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective alt text for meaningful images must convey the same informational purpose the image serves — for an impact report, that means communicating who, what, and why. The 'image of' redundancy is a common craft failure that signals training opportunity, not just compliance gap.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client is producing a 10-minute documentary video about a program beneficiary. The video editor asks whether captions are sufficient for accessibility or whether audio description is also required. How do you advise them?
options: Captions alone meet WCAG 1.2.2 AA for prerecorded video, so audio description is optional and should only be scoped if the client has budget and a known blind or low-vision audience segment.

Captions serve deaf and hard-of-hearing viewers by transcribing audio, while audio description serves blind and low-vision viewers by narrating visual content not conveyed through dialogue — both are required under WCAG 1.2.5 AA for prerecorded synchronized media, and a documentary relying on visual storytelling almost certainly has meaningful visual information that captions alone do not convey.
Audio description is only required under WCAG AAA (1.2.7 Extended Audio Description), so the client can defer it without incurring a AA conformance failure, provided captions are accurate.

Recommend the client produce a separate audio-described version only if the video will be embedded on a government-funded page, since Section 508 triggers audio description requirements that WCAG AA does not.
quizQuestions.explanation: WCAG 1.2.5 (AA) requires audio description for prerecorded video where the visual track carries information not present in the audio — which is almost always true in documentary storytelling. Conflating captions and audio description is the most common gap in nonprofit video production workflows.

quizQuestions.question: You are reviewing auto-generated captions on a nonprofit's YouTube channel. The captions are 85% accurate but contain errors on the executive director's name, the program name, and key statistics. How do you prioritize the remediation case to your client?
options: 85% accuracy is above the industry threshold for acceptable automated captions; recommend the client enable captions on all videos and invest the time saved in producing audio descriptions instead.

Caption accuracy is a matter of legal compliance only if the org receives federal funding — for a privately funded nonprofit, accurate captions are a best practice recommendation rather than a requirement.

Errors on names, branded terms, and statistics are disproportionately harmful because they undermine credibility and cause meaning-critical misunderstandings — not just inconveniences — for deaf and hard-of-hearing users. Recommend editing captions in YouTube's caption editor as a low-cost, high-impact remediation that also improves SEO.

Recommend the client invest in a captioning software subscription to replace YouTube's auto-captions entirely, since automated captions should never be published without human review under WCAG 1.2.2.
quizQuestions.explanation: Accuracy errors on proper nouns and data points compromise the communicative integrity of the content, not just its technical conformance — this framing resonates with communications-minded nonprofit staff. The YouTube editor is a free, accessible fix that also delivers SEO benefit, making it an easy win to recommend.

modules.moduleTitle: Testing and validation tools: from quick checks to CI integration
modules.moduleDescription: Equips the consultant with a practical testing toolkit spanning automated scanning, manual inspection, markup validation, and color contrast analysis. For a UX professional building a consulting practice, knowing which tool catches which class of issues — and which issues no tool can catch — is what separates credible auditors from automated-report resellers.

resources.label: WebAIM Color Contrast Checker
resources.description: An interactive tool that calculates contrast ratios between foreground and background colors to determine WCAG 2.0/2.1 AA and AAA compliance, with immediate pass/fail results for normal text, large text, and UI components.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Color contrast is the most common WCAG failure and the fastest win in any design audit — this is the tool your clients' designers will actually use daily once you show it to them.

resources.label: Colour Contrast Checker – ColourContrast.cc
resources.description: An interactive web tool evaluating color contrast ratios against WCAG standards with hex, RGB, and HSL input options, real-time preview, and pass/fail results for all four WCAG compliance levels.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: The HSL slider interface makes this more intuitive for designers iterating on brand colors in real time — pair it with the WebAIM checker to give clients two tools with different UX affordances for the same critical task.
keyTopics: WCAG contrast standards AA and AAA

resources.label: W3C Markup Validation Service
resources.description: A free tool that checks markup validity of HTML, XHTML, SMIL, MathML, and other formats via URI, file upload, or direct input, reporting character encoding issues, document type conformance, and markup errors.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Invalid markup is a silent accessibility killer that automated WCAG scanners often miss — running the validator is a fast first pass that frequently surfaces the structural problems underlying deeper AT failures.

resources.label: W3C CSS Validation Service
resources.description: A free online tool checking CSS and HTML/XHTML documents with embedded stylesheets for W3C standards compliance, with configurable CSS profile, media type, and warning level options.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: CSS errors can silently break focus visibility, reading order, and zoom behavior — validating stylesheets during an audit rounds out the technical diligence picture and adds credibility to your deliverables.

resources.label: ANDI – Accessibility Testing Tool (SSA)
resources.description: A free browser bookmarklet from the Social Security Administration that automates accessibility testing and Section 508 compliance checking, revealing what screen readers should announce for interactive elements and providing actionable suggestions.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Section 508 compliance language appears in U.S. federal grant requirements that your nonprofit clients must often satisfy — ANDI's direct 508 framing makes it uniquely useful when communicating audit findings in that regulatory context.

resources.label: WAVE Web Accessibility Evaluation Tools
resources.description: A comprehensive suite of accessibility evaluation tools including browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge, subscription APIs, and stand-alone testing engines, designed to identify WCAG errors while facilitating human evaluation.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: WAVE's in-page visual overlay approach is uniquely effective for walking non-technical clients through accessibility issues in real time — it turns an abstract audit report into a live, tangible demonstration.

resources.label: Lighthouse – Google
resources.description: An open-source automated tool for improving web page quality, runnable in Chrome DevTools, from the command line, or as a Node module, with audits covering accessibility, performance, SEO, and more, plus CI integration via Lighthouse CI.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Lighthouse CI integration lets you recommend a continuous accessibility testing workflow to clients' engineering teams, shifting accessibility left in the development process rather than treating it as an end-of-project audit.

quizQuestions.question: Your client's developer ran Lighthouse on the site and it returned an accessibility score of 92. The developer is presenting this as near-complete compliance to the board. How do you reframe this for the client?
options: Affirm the score as a credible baseline — Lighthouse audits align with WCAG AA criteria, so a 92 means the site has minor issues that can be remediated in a single sprint.

Explain that Lighthouse scores measure a weighted subset of automatable checks and cannot evaluate focus management, screen reader announcement quality, keyboard interaction patterns, cognitive load, or most Understandable and Robust criteria — a 92 score with no manual testing is not a compliance statement.

Recommend running axe-core in addition to Lighthouse since the two tools flag different rule sets, and a combined automated score above 90 from both tools is a reasonable proxy for WCAG AA conformance.
Advise the board that Lighthouse scores are valid for Section 508 compliance reporting but should not be cited for ADA or WCAG AA conformance claims without supplemental documentation.
quizQuestions.explanation: Lighthouse catches automatable issues, which represent roughly 20–30% of WCAG failure modes — a high automated score can mask significant barriers invisible to scanners. Correcting this misunderstanding protects the client from false assurance and creates the mandate for a comprehensive manual audit.

quizQuestions.question: You are scoping an accessibility audit for a nonprofit client and need to determine which color combinations on their donation form fail WCAG contrast requirements. Which combination of tool use and judgment is most appropriate?
options: Run the site through an automated scanner such as axe DevTools and rely on its contrast failure flags — automated tools calculate contrast ratios precisely and this step does not require manual verification.

Use a contrast ratio calculator with hex input to evaluate the exact foreground and background values in context, check both normal text (4.5:1) and large text (3:1) thresholds, and separately verify UI component contrast (3:1) for form borders and focus indicators, since each category has a different WCAG threshold.

Sample contrast from the live site using browser DevTools color picker and apply a single 4.5:1 threshold across all elements, since WCAG AA uses a uniform standard for all text and interface components.
Delegate contrast checking to the developer using a browser extension, since precise hex values require access to source CSS that a consultant reviewing a live site cannot reliably extract.
quizQuestions.explanation: WCAG defines distinct contrast thresholds for normal text (4.5:1), large text (3:1), and non-text UI components (3:1 under 1.4.11) — collapsing these into a single ratio is a common auditor error that produces both false positives and false negatives. Knowing which threshold applies where is a core auditing competency.

quizQuestions.question: A client wants to integrate accessibility testing into their CI/CD pipeline so issues are caught before deployment. They ask which tool you recommend and what its limitations are. What is the most accurate and useful answer?
options: Recommend Pa11y or axe-core as a Node module that can fail builds on detectable WCAG violations — and be explicit that CI integration catches automatable issues at commit time but cannot replace manual testing, user testing with disabled participants, or screen reader compatibility checks.

Recommend Deque's axe DevTools Pro because its subscription API enables CI integration and its enterprise support means the client can rely on vendor accountability rather than consultant interpretation for findings.
Advise against CI integration for a small nonprofit because the configuration overhead and false positive rate will overwhelm a resource-constrained development team and create alert fatigue.

Recommend W3C's Nu HTML Checker as the CI integration layer because markup validity is the root cause of most accessibility failures and catching it at commit time eliminates the need for additional accessibility-specific tooling.
quizQuestions.explanation: CI-integrated automated testing is high-leverage precisely because it catches regressions at the point of lowest remediation cost — but scoping it accurately as covering only automatable issues prevents the client from treating passing builds as compliance certification. Setting this expectation correctly is what makes you a trusted advisor rather than a tool vendor.

modules.moduleTitle: Mobile and native app accessibility: iOS, Android, and cross-platform
modules.moduleDescription: Extends accessibility practice into native and hybrid mobile environments, covering platform-specific APIs, screen reader support (VoiceOver, TalkBack), and framework-level implementations in React Native and Flutter. As nonprofits increasingly deliver services through mobile apps and their user bases include people with disabilities relying on mobile AT, consultants who can speak to both web and mobile close more comprehensive engagements.

resources.label: Flutter Accessibility Documentation
resources.description: Flutter's official accessibility documentation covering core principles, regulations, framework support for assistive technologies, UI design patterns, testing approaches, and a release checklist for building accessible apps.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Flutter's cross-platform nature makes it increasingly attractive to resource-constrained nonprofits building one codebase for iOS and Android — understanding its accessibility model lets you evaluate these projects credibly.

resources.label: React Native Accessibility Documentation
resources.description: Comprehensive reference documentation for implementing accessibility in React Native apps across Android and iOS, covering 25+ accessibility properties and APIs for screen reader support (VoiceOver, TalkBack) and assistive technology integration.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: React Native powers a large share of nonprofit mobile apps built by small dev teams — knowing the accessibilityRole and accessibilityLabel API surface lets you write concrete, implementable accessibility requirements for these projects.

resources.label: Apple Human Interface Guidelines – Accessibility
resources.description: Apple's official accessibility design guidelines covering vision, hearing, mobility, speech, and cognitive accessibility across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, visionOS, and watchOS, with specific guidance on Dynamic Type, VoiceOver, Switch Control, and Assistive Access.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Apple's HIG is the gold standard for mobile accessibility design thinking — its POUR-like structure (Perceivable, Adaptable, Intuitive) maps cleanly to your existing WCAG fluency and extends it credibly into the iOS ecosystem.

resources.label: Android Accessibility Developer Guidelines
resources.description: Google's official Android accessibility documentation covering principles for improving app accessibility, including labeling elements, adding accessibility actions, extending system widgets, using non-color cues, and making media accessible, with code examples for TalkBack and Switch Access.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Android's dominant global market share — especially in the Global South where many NGOs operate — makes TalkBack and Switch Access support non-optional for internationally-facing clients; this is the authoritative implementation reference.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client is launching an iOS app that lets beneficiaries schedule services. The development team used custom-drawn UI components throughout for brand consistency. A QA tester reports that VoiceOver announces all custom buttons as 'button' with no additional context. What is the root cause and what do you recommend?
options: The issue is that the team did not set the accessibilityLabel property on custom components — native UIKit elements expose their labels automatically but custom views require explicit accessibilityLabel and accessibilityRole values to be set in code.
The issue is a VoiceOver configuration problem on the test device — custom drawn components require VoiceOver's Custom Actions rotor to be enabled before labels are announced correctly.
The issue is that custom-drawn components cannot be made accessible on iOS and the team should replace them with standard UIKit controls to achieve VoiceOver compatibility.

The issue is that accessibilityHint is missing — VoiceOver announces the role automatically from the view hierarchy but requires accessibilityHint to provide contextual information about button purpose.
quizQuestions.explanation: Custom views do not inherit accessibility metadata from the visual rendering — accessibilityLabel, accessibilityRole, and accessibilityState must all be explicitly set in code for VoiceOver to communicate what the control is, what it is called, and what state it is in. This is the most common gap in custom UI component development.

quizQuestions.question: During a mobile accessibility review of an Android app for a nonprofit's field staff, you find that form fields have visible labels in the UI but TalkBack announces only the input type ('Edit box, double tap to edit'). What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: TalkBack requires a minimum API level 26 to read label associations — the app's minSdkVersion is likely too low and should be updated to resolve the labeling failure.

The visual labels are likely implemented as plain TextView elements that are not programmatically associated with their input fields — adding android:labelFor on the TextView pointing to the corresponding EditText will expose the association to TalkBack and other assistive technologies.

The issue is a contentDescription conflict — TalkBack prioritizes contentDescription over label associations, so the developer should remove all contentDescription values from the form and rely on the label hierarchy.

The fix is to add an accessibilityDelegate to each EditText that overrides onInitializeAccessibilityNodeInfo to inject the label text — this is the recommended Android pattern for associating labels with custom and standard form controls.
quizQuestions.explanation: android:labelFor is the standard Android mechanism for associating visible text labels with form controls in the accessibility tree — without it, TalkBack has no programmatic basis to announce the label and falls back to generic input type announcements. This is the Android equivalent of the HTML label-for pattern.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client is building a React Native app targeting both iOS and Android. The lead developer asks whether they should use React Native's built-in accessibility props or implement platform-specific native modules for VoiceOver and TalkBack support. How do you advise them?
options: Recommend native modules exclusively — React Native's accessibility abstraction layer has documented gaps in VoiceOver and TalkBack support and any app requiring real accessibility compliance should bypass the cross-platform layer.

React Native's accessibility props (accessible, accessibilityLabel, accessibilityRole, accessibilityState) map to both iOS and Android platform APIs and are sufficient for most standard component patterns; platform-specific properties (e.g., iOS accessibilityViewIsModal or Android importantForAccessibility) should be applied where cross-platform props have documented gaps — and AT testing on real devices with VoiceOver and TalkBack is non-negotiable before release.

Advise the client to use aria-* props throughout since React Native's newer architecture aligns with web ARIA semantics and these props have the best long-term support trajectory across both platforms.

Recommend the team defer accessibility implementation until the app is stable in production — React Native's accessibility APIs change frequently enough between versions that implementing them during active development creates maintenance overhead that outweighs early implementation benefits.
quizQuestions.explanation: React Native's cross-platform accessibility props handle the majority of use cases but have documented platform-specific gaps that require targeted native properties — and no accessibility implementation is credible without AT testing on physical devices with actual screen readers. This balanced answer signals both framework fluency and testing discipline.

modules.moduleTitle: Design practice: embedding accessibility in UX and design systems
modules.moduleDescription: Addresses accessibility as a design discipline rather than a development afterthought — covering inclusive design principles, design system integration, and practical design-phase testing. For a senior UX professional with information architecture and design systems fluency, this is the module that translates existing mastery into accessibility leadership, making you the person who changes process upstream rather than fixing issues at QA.

resources.label: Smashing Magazine – Accessibility Articles
resources.description: A curated collection of 161 articles on web accessibility from Smashing Magazine covering inclusive design, assistive technologies, WCAG compliance, accessible UX research, and accessibility in design tools like Figma.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Smashing Magazine bridges the design-development gap better than most a11y resources — its articles on accessible UX research methods and Figma workflows map directly onto the design systems practice you're bringing to this field.
keyTopics: Design for deaf and hard-of-hearing users

resources.label: Carie Fisher – Digital Accessibility Portfolio and Blog
resources.description: Carie Fisher's portfolio and blog focused on digital accessibility education, featuring content on accessible SVG, typography, ARIA implementation, alternative text, multimedia accessibility, and conference inclusion.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Fisher's work on accessible SVG and typography is directly applicable to the icon-heavy, data-visualization-rich digital products common in the nonprofit sector — her practical design focus complements the more standards-heavy resources in this curriculum.
resources.freshnessNote: Copyright 2023 — check that specific articles on SVG and ARIA patterns reflect current browser support, as this space evolves quickly.

resources.label: Web Workshop – Accessible Web Development (Dimensions Project)
resources.description: A structured web development workshop curriculum designed for students who are blind, covering HTML, CSS, and JavaScript with accessibility-first pedagogy, tactile diagrams for visual concepts, and open-source materials including prerecorded MP3 lectures.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Experiencing a curriculum designed entirely around blind learners fundamentally reshapes your mental model of what 'accessible by default' means in practice — this is experiential empathy-building that makes your design recommendations more authentic.
keyTopics: HTML and CSS fundamentals for blind learners
resources.freshnessNote: Last iteration August–October 2020 — curriculum content remains pedagogically valuable but specific tools and code examples may reflect older browser/AT support.

quizQuestions.question: You are leading an accessibility review of a nonprofit's Figma design system before the component library enters development. The design team considers accessibility a 'developer concern.' What is the highest-leverage intervention you can make at this stage?
options: Commission a post-development WCAG audit and share the findings with the design team — real failure data from built components is more persuasive than abstract design-phase recommendations.

Conduct a color contrast review of the design tokens and flag failures — contrast is measurable in Figma and is the most common design-phase error, making it the clearest entry point for establishing accessibility review as a design-stage practice.

Annotate Figma frames with ARIA roles, focus order, and accessible name requirements for each component — surfacing accessibility specifications in the design file reframes them as design decisions rather than developer afterthoughts and embeds the practice in existing workflow.

Recommend the design team complete WCAG 2.1 training before the component library proceeds — without foundational knowledge, design-phase annotations will not be interpreted or applied correctly by the team.
quizQuestions.explanation: Accessibility annotations in Figma transform accessibility from a vague developer responsibility into a specified design output — they create shared vocabulary, make expectations auditable, and upstream the conversation before code is written. This is the process-change intervention that separates an accessibility leader from a compliance auditor.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client's new donation form uses a CAPTCHA to prevent spam. Blind users on your advisory panel report they cannot complete the form. The development team suggests adding an audio CAPTCHA alternative. How do you evaluate this recommendation?
options: Audio CAPTCHA is the WCAG-compliant alternative to visual CAPTCHA and resolves the accessibility barrier — recommend the team implement it and document conformance under success criterion 1.1.1.

Audio CAPTCHA is a recognized but deeply problematic alternative — it remains inaccessible to users who are both deaf and blind, is notoriously difficult to parse for users with cognitive or processing differences, and creates a degraded experience. Recommend evaluating non-CAPTCHA spam prevention alternatives such as honeypot fields or behavioral analysis that remove the barrier entirely.

Recommend the team implement audio CAPTCHA as an interim measure while scoping a longer-term migration to a fully accessible alternative — this satisfies WCAG conformance while a better solution is designed.

CAPTCHA accessibility is an unresolved problem that WCAG explicitly acknowledges cannot be fully solved — the client should add an accessible contact method as a manual bypass and document the exception in their accessibility conformance report.
quizQuestions.explanation: Audio CAPTCHA is widely recognized as an inadequate alternative that fails users with deafblindness and cognitive disabilities while degrading experience for all users — the stronger consulting recommendation is to eliminate the CAPTCHA barrier rather than replace one inaccessible implementation with another. This is where inclusive design thinking outperforms compliance-minimum thinking.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit design director argues that using a small, light-weight font throughout the site is a brand requirement that cannot be changed for accessibility. How do you navigate this objection while preserving the client relationship?
options: Accept the brand constraint and recommend compensating through increased line height and letter spacing, which can improve readability without altering the typeface or weight.

Document the decision as a known WCAG 1.4.3 and 1.4.4 risk, recommend the client obtain legal sign-off, and move on — brand decisions are outside the consultant's scope when the client has been clearly informed.

Reframe the conversation: demonstrate that WCAG 1.4.4 requires text to be resizable to 200% without loss of content, that font weight and size interact with color contrast ratios under 1.4.3, and that many type systems achieve brand sophistication through spacing, layout, and hierarchy rather than small light type — offering to facilitate a design exploration that preserves brand intent while meeting accessibility requirements.
Escalate to the executive director and frame the brand override as a legal liability that the design director is not authorized to accept on behalf of the organization.
quizQuestions.explanation: The most effective consultant response to brand-versus-accessibility conflicts is a design reframe rather than a legal threat or a capitulation — demonstrating that accessibility constraints are design constraints that skilled designers solve is what builds trust and changes culture. Escalation and documentation-only responses both damage the relationship without solving the problem.

modules.moduleTitle: The a11y professional ecosystem: community, consultancies, and continuing education
modules.moduleDescription: Maps the landscape of organizations, practitioners, and training platforms that constitute the professional accessibility community. For a consultant building a practice, social capital within the a11y community is as important as technical knowledge — knowing who the credible voices are, which organizations to partner with, and where ongoing education happens is what makes your practice sustainable.

resources.label: Deque University
resources.description: A comprehensive digital accessibility training platform offering self-paced courses, instructor-led training, and reference materials for all skill levels, covering web, document, and mobile accessibility and IAAP certification preparation.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: IAAP CPACC certification is the professional credential most commonly requested in accessibility consulting RFPs — Deque University is the most established prep pathway and signals serious professional commitment to prospective clients.

resources.label: TetraLogical
resources.description: An accessibility consultancy specializing in inclusive design and digital accessibility, offering assessments, strategy, development, and team training across knowledge, experience, and organizational levels.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: TetraLogical's blog and public resources represent some of the most technically rigorous practitioner output in the field — following their work keeps your methodology current and helps you understand how top-tier consulting engagements are structured.

resources.label: Easy Surf Canada
resources.description: A Vancouver-based digital accessibility consulting firm specializing in websites and video games, founded by and staffed with international experts who have lived experience with disabilities, providing actionable insights from prototype to production.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: Easy Surf's model of centering disabled experts throughout the engagement — not just as testers — is a differentiator worth studying as you define your own consulting methodology and team-building approach.

resources.label: Bureau of Internet Accessibility (BoIA)
resources.description: A 21-year-old accessibility consulting firm offering WCAG 2.1 A/AA audits, compliance roadmaps, remediation, training, and automated testing tools, serving 72 countries with 157 accessibility professionals.
resources.whyThisWhyNow: BoIA's scope, audit methodology, and compliance documentation services define the competitive landscape you're entering — studying their service model helps you position your boutique nonprofit-focused practice against full-service firms.
resources.freshnessNote: Copyright 2023 noted in footer — verify current service offerings and pricing directly, as the accessibility services market has evolved significantly.

quizQuestions.question: A potential nonprofit client asks whether your accessibility consulting credentials include formal certification. You hold a CPACC credential from IAAP. How do you position this in the context of the broader a11y professional ecosystem?
options: Present CPACC as the definitive credential in the field — its IAAP backing and international recognition make it the strongest possible signal of accessibility competence to a nonprofit client.

Present CPACC as foundational conceptual certification that demonstrates grounding in disability, assistive technology, and international standards, while being transparent that technical practice depth is demonstrated through audit portfolios, client outcomes, and community contributions — the credential is a floor, not a ceiling.

Defer the credentials question and redirect to portfolio case studies — nonprofit clients are not familiar with IAAP credentialing and the time spent explaining it is better spent demonstrating impact.

Recommend the client verify your credentials through IAAP's public registry and also ask any candidate they evaluate about their WAS (Web Accessibility Specialist) certification, which is the technical credential that validates implementation expertise.
quizQuestions.explanation: Overclaiming credential authority undermines credibility with sophisticated clients; underclaiming it wastes a genuine signal. The honest framing — CPACC as conceptual foundation, supplemented by demonstrated practice — is both accurate and positions you as a thoughtful professional rather than a credential collector.

quizQuestions.question: You are building your nonprofit accessibility consulting practice and want to establish credibility within the a11y professional community. Which approach generates the most durable social capital with the people who influence purchasing and referral decisions?
options: Publish a weekly LinkedIn newsletter summarizing WCAG updates and accessibility news — consistent content output builds audience and positions you as a thought leader to procurement-adjacent audiences.
Pursue WCAG 2.1 audit contracts with established agencies as a subcontractor, using the volume of completed audits to build a verifiable portfolio before approaching nonprofits directly.

Contribute substantively to the community — whether through writing, speaking at accessibility conferences, participating in W3C working groups, mentoring newer practitioners, or contributing to open resources — while being generous with knowledge in the spaces where credible a11y practitioners congregate.
Partner with a disability-led consultancy as a white-label delivery partner — associating your practice with disability representation in tech is the most credible signal in the current a11y market.
quizQuestions.explanation: The a11y community is tight-knit and referral-driven — social capital is built through genuine contribution and knowledge generosity, not content marketing or credential accumulation. Practitioners who shape community thinking through teaching, writing, and collaboration are the ones whose names circulate when nonprofit RFPs go out.

quizQuestions.question: A nonprofit client asks you to recommend an accessibility training vendor for their 15-person product team. They want to know how to evaluate vendors beyond marketing claims. What criteria do you recommend?
options: Recommend evaluating vendors by their WCAG audit volume and the number of IAAP-certified trainers on staff — these are the two most reliable proxies for training quality in the current market.

Recommend they prioritize vendors who offer CPACC exam prep as part of their curriculum, since certification-aligned training ensures the content meets a validated standard rather than a proprietary framework.

Recommend evaluating whether the training includes disabled instructors or advisors, whether curriculum covers application to their specific tech stack, whether it differentiates by role (design vs. engineering vs. content), and whether there is follow-up support — generic compliance training rarely changes team practice without role-specific application and ongoing coaching.

Recommend the client use WebAIM's training resources as a cost-effective baseline, supplemented by a custom workshop you deliver — this combination provides credibility through a recognized nonprofit organization and practical application through direct consultation.
quizQuestions.explanation: Training that changes practice must be role-differentiated, applied to real work, and ideally informed by disabled practitioners — generic WCAG overview courses produce credential-holding employees who still produce inaccessible work. These evaluative criteria help the client distinguish transformative training from compliance theater.

crossCuttingThemes: WCAG as both a compliance standard and a design framework
The gap between automated testing and genuine AT user experience
Disability justice as a frame beyond legal compliance
Semantic HTML as the foundational layer beneath all accessibility interventions
Community consensus versus vendor marketing claims
Accessibility as organizational culture change, not one-time remediation
Cross-platform consistency challenges across web, iOS, and Android
Business and mission case for accessibility in resource-constrained organizations
notableGaps: Cognitive and neurodivergent accessibility — WCAG 2.x covers this poorly and the resources above do not address it systematically despite being highly relevant to nonprofits serving diverse populations
PDF and document accessibility — a major pain point for nonprofits with large report and grant documentation libraries, mentioned briefly in Deque University but not addressed by a dedicated resource
Accessibility in data visualization and infographics — critical for impact reporting and advocacy materials but absent from this resource set
Procurement and vendor accessibility evaluation — how to assess third-party tools and platforms for accessibility compliance, essential for consultants advising on software selection
WCAG 3.0 / APCA (Accessible Perceptual Contrast Algorithm) developments — the next generation of contrast standards is in active development and will affect design system recommendations
Internationalization and multilingual accessibility — directly relevant for NGOs operating in multiple languages and regions
Accessible content management systems — practical guidance for which CMS platforms support accessibility best, relevant for most nonprofit website projects

User testing with disabled participants — methodology for recruiting, facilitating, and compensating disabled research participants, which is how accessibility recommendations gain real-world grounding

failed.label: Magenta A11y
failed.errorReason: Page is a single-page application (SPA) with minimal HTML content. Actual content loads dynamically via JavaScript. Fetched HTML contains only metadata, framework initialization, and an empty root div—insufficient substantive text for analysis.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://www.magentaa11y.com in your browser. Review the main content about accessibility criteria and tools. Copy the descriptive body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/magenta-a11y.txt

failed.label: FreeCodeCamp.org - Learn how to code HTML, CSS, and JS
failed.errorReason: Page content consists primarily of CSS styling code and HTML structure with minimal substantive text content visible in the fetched markup. No readable educational content about courses, curriculum, or learning resources is present in the extracted page content.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://www.freecodecamp.org in your browser. Review the main course offerings and educational content. Copy the descriptive body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/freecodecamp.txt

failed.label: W3Schools - Learn HTML, CSS, and JS
failed.errorReason: Page content is primarily navigation structure and metadata. The provided HTML is truncated and lacks the main tutorial body content that would typically follow the navigation sections.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://www.w3schools.com/html/ in your browser. Review the HTML tutorial introduction and curriculum overview. Copy the descriptive body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/w3schools-html.txt

failed.label: WCAG of the Day - Digestible WCAG, daily email education
failed.errorReason: URL mismatch: The requested URL (dwcag.org) does not match the fetched content, which is from a Russian-language online casino (Vavada). The page returned is in Russian and entirely unrelated to WCAG resources. This appears to be a domain hijacking, content injection, or incorrect URL submission.
failed.manualInstructions: Verify the correct URL for 'WCAG of the Day'. If the domain has changed or been compromised, update the URL in the source list. If you have the correct URL, visit it in your browser, copy the main content describing the daily WCAG email service, and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/wcag-of-the-day.txt

failed.label: Jennifer Sutton on Twitter
failed.manualInstructions: Visit http://twitter.com/jsutt in your browser. If the Twitter profile loads, review Jennifer Sutton's recent accessibility-related posts and bio. Copy relevant content and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/jennifer-sutton-twitter.txt. If the URL is no longer valid, consider replacing it with an alternative profile or resource.

failed.label: Overlay Fact Sheet - All you need to fight accessibility overlay companies and products
failed.errorReason: Page contains only a redirect meta tag with no substantive content. HTML meta refresh redirects to https://overlayfactsheet.com/en/ without providing readable content.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://overlayfactsheet.com/en/ (the redirect destination) in your browser. Review the fact sheet content about accessibility overlays. Copy the main body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/overlay-fact-sheet.txt

failed.label: WebAccessibilitySurvey.com - Survey Data from Assistive Technology Users
failed.errorReason: Page content is primarily HTML/CSS boilerplate and styling declarations. The actual body content is not included in the fetched page_content; only the <head> section and extensive inline CSS are present.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://webaccessibilitysurvey.com/ in your browser. Review the survey overview, data findings, and purpose. Copy the descriptive body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/web-accessibility-survey.txt

failed.label: Apple's Human Interface Guidelines on Accessibility
failed.errorReason: Page loaded but contains only JavaScript-rendered content with no substantive text visible in the HTML source. The page requires client-side JavaScript execution to display content, and the fetched HTML only includes metadata and a loading placeholder.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/accessibility in your browser. Review Apple's accessibility design guidelines for iOS and macOS. Copy the main content describing accessibility principles and implementation guidance, then paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/apple-accessibility-guidelines.txt

failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://developer.android.com/guide/topics/ui/accessibility/principles in your browser. Review Android's accessibility design principles and implementation guidelines. Copy the main body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/android-accessibility-principles.txt

failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://www.magentaa11y.com/#/native-criteria/controls/overview in your browser. Review the native app accessibility criteria and control guidelines. Copy the main content and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/magenta-a11y-native-criteria.txt

failed.errorReason: Page content is primarily JavaScript/framework scaffolding with minimal substantive HTML text visible. The fetched HTML contains mostly script tags, CSS, and configuration code rather than readable page content.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://www.deque.com/get-started-axe-devtools-browser-extension/ in your browser. Review the Axe DevTools overview, features, and installation instructions. Copy the main body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/axe-devtools-browser-extension.txt

Review Lighthouse's accessibility testing features and overview. Copy the main body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/google-lighthouse.txt

failed.label: International Association of Accessibility Professionals, or IAAP
failed.errorReason: Page content is primarily JavaScript application framework code and configuration. No substantive HTML content visible in the fetched markup—only LWR (Lightning Web Runtime) framework initialization and route definitions.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://www.accessibilityassociation.org/s/ in your browser. Review the IAAP homepage, mission, membership information, and resources. Copy the main descriptive content and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/iaap.txt

failed.label: Deque
failed.errorReason: Page content is heavily obscured by script tags and minimal visible text. The fetched HTML is dominated by analytics, tracking scripts, and configuration code with no substantive accessibility content visible in the rendered text.
failed.manualInstructions: Visit https://www.deque.com in your browser. Review Deque's company overview, services, products (axe, training, consulting), and mission. Copy the main body text and paste it into data/accessibility-links-and-resources/manual/deque.txt

failed.label: The Paciello Group International - TPGI
